response:
# Blog Article Page Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users, keyboard-only users, low vision users, cognitive accessibility users > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation **Feature:** A static long-form blog article page rendered from Drupal content through a React/Next.js front end. **Risk Level:** Low to Medium **Component/Page Type:** Editorial article page, static reading experience --- ## Scope & Context This plan covers the full article page structure: site header, primary navigation, skip link, main article content, author/date metadata, CMS-rendered body content, images, blockquote, complementary related-articles sidebar, and footer. There are no custom widgets, overlays, live regions, focus traps, menus, tabs, or ARIA composite patterns. The risk comes from semantic degradation caused by `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, CMS-authored headings, universal empty image alt text, and reuse of the same Article component for teaser and full-page contexts. This plan is **not** claiming to solve editorial quality at the content strategy level. It defines the accessibility implementation contract and CMS authoring guardrails needed so the rendered page exposes correct structure and relationships. Primary WCAG criteria: - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships - WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence - WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text - WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow - WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks - WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose - WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ## Semantic Structure Plan Use native HTML landmarks. Do not add ARIA roles where native elements already provide the correct semantics. ### Structure Diagram ```html Skip to main content

Article title

By Author Name

Published May 15, 2026

Related articles

``` ### Landmark Rules - `
`: site masthead and primary navigation. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships. - `
`: primary site navigation. Use `aria-label` because there may be multiple navigation landmarks. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1 and 4.1.2. - Skip link: first focusable element, targets `
`. Satisfies WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks. - `
`: unique main content landmark. Only one per page. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1. - `
`: standalone article content. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1. - Article `
`: article-specific title and metadata, distinct from site header. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1. - `
`: complementary related content. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1. - `
`: if related links are navigational, label this nav distinctly from primary and footer nav. Satisfies WCAG 4.1.2. - `
` with `
`: site-wide footer links. Satisfies WCAG 1.3.1 and 4.1.2. --- ## Heading Hierarchy Strategy Document outline rule: - `h1`: article title only. - `h2`: major article sections. - `h3`: subsections inside the preceding `h2`. - Do not skip from `h1` to `h3`. - Do not choose heading levels based on visual size. - Visual design tokens must be decoupled from semantic heading level. WCAG citations: - WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships: heading structure must communicate content relationships programmatically. - WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels: headings must describe topic or purpose. ### Drupal/React Repair Approach Because headings are CMS-authored and currently visually driven: 1. Full article page must enforce one page-level `h1` from the article title. 2. CMS body content should begin at `h2`, not `h1`. 3. If Drupal body contains additional `h1` elements, transform them to `h2` during sanitized rendering or flag them in editorial validation. 4. If CMS body skips levels, such as `h2` to `h4`, either repair to the nearest valid level during rendering or block publication with an editorial validation message. 5. Teaser/listing mode must not reuse the full article `h1`. Teaser titles should match the listing page outline, usually `h2` or `h3` depending on the parent page. This is where confidence drops if the CMS allows arbitrary HTML without validation. Rendering repair can reduce harm, but editorial training or CMS field constraints are the stronger long-term fix. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design No APG widget patterns apply. This is a static reading page. ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard Model | ARIA Attributes | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Skip link | Native link, no APG pattern | Tab focuses; Enter activates and moves to main target | none required beyond `href="#main-content"` | WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks, 2.1.1 Keyboard | | Primary nav links | Native links, no APG pattern | Tab through links; Enter follows link | `aria-label` on `
` if multiple navs exist | WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.4, 4.1.2 | | Author link | Native link, no APG pattern | Tab focuses; Enter follows link | none if text is descriptive | WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose | | Related article links | Native links, no APG pattern | Tab through links; Enter follows link | parent nav labeled `aria-label="Related articles"` | WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.4, 4.1.2 | | Footer links | Native links, no APG pattern | Tab through links; Enter follows link | parent nav labeled `aria-label="Footer navigation"` | WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.4, 4.1.2 | Do not invent menu, tree, tab, disclosure, or live-region behavior for this page. --- ## Focus Management Plan Focus is a linear reading/navigation path. Expected tab order: 1. Skip link. 2. Header primary navigation links. 3. Author link, if present. 4. Any links inside article body in DOM order. 5. Related article links. 6. Footer links. Focus rules: - Skip link must become visible on focus and move the user to `
`. WCAG 2.4.1. - DOM order must match visual reading order: header, main article, related content, footer. WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence. - No focus trap is needed. - No focus restoration is needed. - No programmatic focus movement is needed after page load, unless this is a client-side route transition. For Next.js client-side navigation, move focus to the article `h1` or `
` after route change so screen reader users know the page changed. WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order. - Focus indicators must be visible with at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible and WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance. - Sticky headers must not obscure focused links or headings. WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured. --- ## State Communication Design There are no expanded, selected, pressed, checked, invalid, busy, or live-updating states in the static article view. ### State Communication Table | State | Visual Indicator | Programmatic Indicator | ARIA Attribute | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Current page in primary nav, if shown | Text, underline, or persistent marker; not color alone | Link text and optional current-page state | `aria-current="page"` on the current nav link | WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color, 4.1.2 Name Role Value | | Decorative image | Image may be visible but not meaningful | Empty alt removes it from screen reader output | `alt=""` | WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content | | Informative image | Image supports article meaning | Descriptive alternative text | `alt="[contextual description]"` | WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content | | Complex image | Chart/diagram/map visible in article | Short alt plus nearby long description or caption association | `aria-describedby` only when needed | WCAG 1.1.1, 1.3.1 | | Publication date | Human-readable date | Machine-readable date | `` | WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships | --- ## Image Alt Text Strategy Universal `alt=""` is wrong for this page because body images vary in purpose. Use this decision tree: 1. Decorative image Use `alt=""`. Applies when the image adds no information not already present in text, such as ornamental separators or mood imagery. WCAG 1.1.1. 2. Contextual/informative image Use concise descriptive alt text. Example: `alt="Volunteers sorting donated books at a community center"` The alt should describe the information the image contributes in this article, not generic visual trivia. WCAG 1.1.1. 3. Image already fully described by adjacent text Use `alt=""` if the nearby prose or caption fully conveys the same information. Avoid duplicate announcements. WCAG 1.1.1. 4. Complex image, chart, diagram, map, infographic Use short alt text plus a long description in adjacent text, caption, or an element referenced with `aria-describedby`. WCAG 1.1.1 and 1.3.1. 5. Linked image If an image is inside a link, its accessible name must describe the link destination or action. If the link has visible text that already names the destination, the image can use `alt=""`. WCAG 2.4.4. CMS requirement: - Drupal image fields must expose an alt text field and an image purpose selector: decorative, informative, complex. - Editorial staff should not be asked only “Alt text?” without context. That produces dead compliance text. - Decorative images must be intentionally marked decorative, not silently defaulted to empty alt. --- ## Blockquote Semantics Use native `
` for quoted material. If the quote has an attribution, include it with visible text near the quote, preferably using `
